coredata.dc:title: A facile approach to synthesize carbon quantum dots with pH-dependent properties
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Chih-Wei
Hsieh, Meng-Lin
Liu, Wei-Ren
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we propose an innovative and environmental-friendly bottom-up synthetic strategy to obtain emission-tunable carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The corresponding characterizations of CQDs, such as TEM, FITR, UV–Visible spectrometer, XPS as well as PL/PLE and decay lifetime are systematically carried out in this study. TEM result demonstrates the particle size of as-synthesized CQDs is 2.4 nm. The emission wavelength of CQDs is 460 nm in acid condition and 550 nm in base condition. The nature of pH dependence for our CQDs might be due to the contents and sorts of functional groups onto CQDs by XPS, UV–Visible spectra and FTIR analyses. The properties of controllable emission could be a potential for sensor applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Photoluminescence
Luminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CQDs. Inset: Particle size distribution of CQDs. (b) HRTEM image of CQDs.
(a) UV–Vis spectrum of CQDs. Insert: digital photograph of CQDs in daylight and under 365 nm UV radiation. (b) PL spectrum of CQDs excited with different wavelength from 320 nm to 420 nm.
The photographs of CQDs solution under (a) daylight and (b) 365 nm UV lamp. PL spectra of (c) 10-CQDs (d) 20-CQDs (e) 30-CQDs (f) 40-CQDs at excitation wavelength starting from 320 nm to 400 nm.
XPS analysis of C 1s spectrum of 20-CQDs, 30-CQDs and 40-CQDs.
The digital photographs of CQDs PL performance with various pH condition. Under (a) daylight (b) UV lamp (λ = 365 nm).
The excitation dependence investigation for CQDs in different pH condition. (f) Irradiated by 365 nm UV lamp.
UV–Vis spectrum of CQDs solution in different pH conditions.
The FTIR spectra of CQDs under different pH environment.
The XPS of C 1s spectrum of CQDs in each examination.
The possible reaction mechanism of carbon quantum dots in this study.
XPS quantitative analysis of C 1s spectra of each CQDs sample.
XPS quantitative analysis of C 1s spectra of CQDs in different pH condition.
Summary of reported literature about carbon quantum dots.

head.ce:title: A facile approach to synthesize carbon quantum dots with pH-dependent properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, R&D Research Center for Membrane Technology, Research Center for Circular Economy, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan City, Chung Li District, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: R&D Research Center for Membrane Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, R&D Research Center for Membrane Technology, Research Center for Circular Economy, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan City, Chung Li District, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we propose an innovative and environmental-friendly bottom-up synthetic strategy to obtain emission-tunable carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The corresponding characterizations of CQDs, such as TEM, FITR, UV–Visible spectrometer, XPS as well as PL/PLE and decay lifetime are systematically carried out in this study. TEM result demonstrates the particle size of as-synthesized CQDs is 2.4 nm. The emission wavelength of CQDs is 460 nm in acid condition and 550 nm in base condition. The nature of pH dependence for our CQDs might be due to the contents and sorts of functional groups onto CQDs by XPS, UV–Visible spectra and FTIR analyses. The properties of controllable emission could be a potential for sensor applications.

ce:section.ce:para: In past decades, novel carbon nanomaterial has attracted much attention since its practical natures [1–3], such as carbon nanofiber, carbon nanotube, graphene and carbon quantum dots (carbon dots, CQDs), most of them had been proved to be a promising material in many research fields including energy storage, lighting, photocatalysts and bioapplications [4–9], the sequence of innovation of carbon nanomaterials could be regarded as an announcement of material science to next generation. The emerging optoelectronic material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a novel quasi-spherical nanoparticle usually with diameter below 20 nm, as a new member of the carbon nanomaterial family, CQDs have been investigated vigorously since 2004 [10], because of its unique chemical and physical properties including wildly carbon sources, long working lifetime, low energy consumption, strong photoluminescence (PL), low cytotoxicity and so on [11–14].

In view of aforementioned advantages, CQDs has shown a wild range of potential substantive applications in many fields such as bioimaing, environment detection, photocatalysis, energy storage, nano-medicine and light-emitting diodes technologies [13–24], furthermore, compared with conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [25–27], the luminescence stability, variety of carbon sources, low cost and non-toxic features let CQDs be deemed to an ideal green optoelectronic material to next generation. So far, many kinds of synthetic approaches have been reported including hydrothermal reactions [28–30], microwave irradiation [31,32], laser ablation [33,34], pyrolysis [35], electrochemical synthesis [36] and ultrasonic treatment [37]. However, most of these approaches require many distinctive instruments and extreme operating conditions like high temperature and pressure or radiative environment might have the misgiving of safety and also cause huge energy waste which will limit further applications and the possibility of producing CQDs in industrial process. Thus, to develop a facile and rapid synthesized strategy also maintain the luminescence properties at same time is inevitable in the future. In the other hand, the PL mechanism of CQDs still remain ambiguous, since in each studies, CQDs were made from different ingredients by kinds of approaches to obtain CQDs with various chemical structure and composition, it is a formidable mission to organize all results into a unity theorem to explain the mechanism clearly, nevertheless we consider a multicolor emission CQDs prepare method to speculate a possible PL mechanism.

As of nowadays, a few studies reported a synthesize approach to produce an emission tunable CQDs, among those works, the possible influence for PL red shift including particle size, pH environment, reaction time and element doping. For instance, Zhang et al. reported two types of CQDs with excitation-independent blue emission and excitation-dependent full-color emission CQDs prepared via a mild process with different reaction time, the research results indicated that both particle size and surface state will cause the result of PL red shift [38]. Xiong et al. produced a full-color light-emitting CQDs by silica column chromatography to obtain CQDs with similar particle size but different polarity, reveal that the degree of surface oxidation has a direct correlation to the PL red shift phenomenon, excluding the influence of quantum size effect [39]. Lin et al. synthesized three kinds of CQDs with blue, green and red emission by using three isomers of phenylenediamines and ascribe the PL red shift to the nitrogen content and quantum size effect [40]. Liu and colleagues prepared an N, S co-doped CQDs, and found that PL wavelength is related to the pH value of environment which cause the change of surface state [41]. So far, two possible mechanisms have been discussing for long time, one is the quantum size effect, and other is surface statement of CQDs. Therefore, a tunable emission wavelength CQDs synthesized with comparable experimental parameters would be useful to help us to discover a convincing PL mechanism, in order to get full color emission CQDs especially red emission for further applications.

Herein, we present a facile and mild synthesized approach to prepare CQDs with tunable emission color and pH dependence property arising from ethanol. In our work, we focus on the fluorescence behavior of CQDs under different condition including reaction time and pH value of environment. We found that as the reaction time and pH value increase, the emission wavelength is changed from 453 to 590 nm and 494–546 nm respectively. By comprehensive investigation and comparison of the as-prepared CQDs, a reasonable conclusion was elicited that the red shift phenomena were ascribed to variation of functional groups onto the CQDs surface, such as C=C, C=O and –OH could be offering different surface state, which lead to difference of band gap for electron transition or defect for capture excitons, resulting the continuous variation in emission wavelength.

The CQDs was synthesized through a novel and facile approach by mixing NaOH and ethanol in a beaker equipped with a magnetic stirrer, heated to 70oC react for 60 min. During heating process, the solution color could be easily observed that varying from transparent to pale brown, indicating the CQDs was gradually formed. The obtained brown product was cooled down to room temperature naturally, and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove non-fluorescent deposit. Finally, a clear yellow solution was obtained. The product was filtered by syringe filters (pore size: 0.22 μm) before further characterizations.

The pH value of CQDs solution was tuned by HCl solution (37%), then centrifuge at 2000 rpm for 5 min to remove precipitation. The environment pH condition of CQDs solution was controlled between 13 and 1, measured by glass electrode pH sensor.

The particle morphologies were investigated by using Transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi S-4800). Horiba Jobin-Yvon FluoroMax-4 spectrometer was selected to measure the photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra. The ultraviolet–visible (UV/Visible) spectra were recorded by using Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer. The chemical bonding statement of CQDs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI model 5802) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscope (FTIR, Bruker TENSOR 27).

According to previous report, the CQDs could be prepared from abundant carbon source [42,43]. Here, we propose a one-step, economical and mass producible synthetic route to obtain CQDs from ethanol through a safe react process by using hot plate instead of traditional poly (para-phenol)-lined stainless steel autoclave, which used for hydrothermal reaction. The as-prepared CQDs exhibits stable yellow fluorescence when exposing under UV lamp (λ = 365 nm). As shown in Fig. 1 (a) and (b), TEM and HRTEM images reveal that the morphology of most CQDs were quasi spherical without obvious lattice fringes and well dispersed in ethanol, this outcome indicates that we synthesized CQDs from ethanol successfully. The size distribution was investigated by choosing about 300 particles randomly, and from particle size distribution histogram shown in inset of Fig. 1(a), the CQDs size was in the range of 1–4 nm with an average diameter of 2.4 nm, compare to the results of previous articles it's much smaller than the CQDs with similar PL properties. Thus, we obviate the relationship between particle size and emission wavelength in our report.

The PL, PLE and UV–Vis spectrum of CQDs were measured to investigate PL properties of CQDs, as presented in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2(a) shows the UV–Vis spectrum of CQDs. The inset displays images of CQD under daylight and 365 nm excitation in UV box. The UV–Vis spectrum reveals two shoulder peaks at 267 nm and 360 nm, which is attributed to π-π* transition of the conjugated C=C onto CQDs surface and n-π* transition of aromatic structure of CQDs, respectively. The maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were 400 nm and 590 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The PLE spectrum depicts a single peak around 395 nm, and the excited-dependence emission trait was observed obviously, the maximum emission peak blue shift as excitation wavelength gradually increase from 320 to 420 nm, according to previous articles, this phenomena might cause by the various surface state or functional group onto the CQDs surface, we will expand more details on upcoming sections.

During reaction period, CQDs was formed through the self-assembly process of ethanol molecular, the solution color change gradually from transparent to pale yellow, as shown in Fig. 3 (a), the CQDs samples synthesized with 10 min–40 min, was labeled as 10-CQDs, 20-CQDs, 30-CQDs and 40-CQDs for simplified our investigation.

Interestingly, we found that as reaction in progress, the emission wavelength of CQDs was synchronized with reaction time. (Fig. 3(b)). From PL spectrum shown in Fig. 3(c) ∼ 3(f), as reaction time increase, the CQDs excited by 400 nm shows distinct excitation dependent property progressively, emission peak of 400 nm excitation shifted from 453 nm to 590 nm. However, the PL spectrum of 10-CQDs and 20-CQDs do not have obvious excitation dependence until 30-CQDs, the excitation dependence started to be observed gradually. It is a significant result for propose to clarify relationship between emission wavelength and surface state of CQDs surface. The UV–Vis spectrum was employed to investigate the energy absorption of CQDs and the surface chemical composition were studied by XPS and FTIR. The UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 4 ) revealed that the lower energy absorption bands of the CQDs shifted towards longer wavelength from 265 nm (40-CQDs) to 303 nm (10-CQDs). The red shift phenomena of the electronic absorption transitions confirmed that the 40-CQDs contained smaller electronic band gap than 10-CQDs [41].

On the other hand, the analysis result of FTIR was shown in Fig. 6 , which elaborated that all samples possessed various functional groups, including the characteristic absorption bands at 3330 cm−1, 2975 cm−1, 2891 cm−1, 1450 cm−1 and 1670 cm−1, were corresponded to –OH, C=C, C-H and C=O stretching vibration, and 1380 cm−1 was C-H bending vibration, respectively. FTIR spectrum indicated that CQDs synthesized from longer reaction time has higher degree of oxidation, and the UV–Vis investigation shows that CQDs with different energy band gap caused the emission peak shifting, because those functional groups will provide abundant structure configurations and introduce new energy levels to CQDs, consequently result in more electronic transition possibilities.

Since CQDs basically composed of amorphous carbon bonded oxygen atoms, we conjectured the emission color has highly related to the degree of oxidative functional group or conjugate bonding, this conclusion was in accord with the previous report also [44,45].

As we concluded in the previous section, the emission wavelength of CQDs red shift was originated from difference of energy band gap due to increment of oxidation functional groups onto CQDs surface. Therefore, if we could control the degree of oxidation or surface state of CQDs by tuning pH value of surrounding environment so that we could forecast the emission peak red shifting phenomena of CQDs will happened.

This feature made obtained CQDs be expected to be utilized as a convenient pH probe. The pH dependence trait of CQDs was investigated by dropping CQDs solution into 5 beakers with aqueous solution which pH value was 1, 5, 7, 9 and 13, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7 , after dropping the CQDs solution into each solution, the solution color still remains in similar color without obvious difference, but the emission color under illumination of UV-light (λ = 365 nm) changed instantly and dramatically from yellow to blue. The CQDs solution exhibited an impressive performance of pH dependence trait, which emission color was varying from bright yellow to blue when the pH value alternately controlled from alkaline to acid environment. The PL spectrum of each sample was fully investigated as shown in Fig. 8 . For the convenience of practical operation, the emission behavior of each solution under irradiation of 365 nm was analyzed in Fig. 8(f), each emission peak were 546 nm, 524 nm, 524 nm, 520 nm and 495 nm, respectively. Obviously, at weak base and acid environment (pH 5–9), the emission wavelength remains in green region (519 nm ∼ 524 nm) without obvious red shifting phenomena, the same result was founded in UV–Vis spectrum in Fig. 9 , the second shoulder peak to each sample was 355 nm, 376 nm, 376 nm, 374 nm and 410 nm, respectively. Again, UV–Vis spectrum revealed that the lower energy absorption bands of the CQDs shifted towards longer wavelength from 355 nm (pH 13) to 410 nm (pH 1). Thus, the electronic absorption transitions confirmed that the CQDs in base environment contained smaller electronic band gap than in acid environment. The functional groups composition of the each CQDs sample was carried out by FTIR spectrum (Fig. 10 ), the characteristic absorption bands at 3386 cm−1, 2975 cm−1, 1649 cm−1 and 1047 cm−1 were corresponded to –OH, C-H, C=O and C-O stretching vibration, respectively. To confirm the results of FTIR and our hypothesis of red shift phenomena, the XPS analysis was employed also. As shown in Fig. 11
, the high resolution scan of the C 1s region was divided into three characteristic peaks obtained by deconvolution: 284.88 eV, 286.48 eV and 288.38 eV, which were corresponds to C=C, C-O and –COOH. From quantitative analysis of XPS (Table 2 ), demonstrating that compared to percentage –COOH in acid solution was 4.69%, quantity of –COOH were arising to 11.06% as treated in alkaline solution, the carboxyl group of CQDs in acid solution was less than in base condition, it was in conformity with FTIR results and our hypothesis about the band gap was to be regarded as a dominant factor to red shift phenomena, which could be controlled by adjusting the amount of oxidation functional groups onto CQDs surface. Eventually, the difference of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest occupied molecular orbital (LOMO), it has obvious influence to the emission wavelength of CQDs, leading to the red shift phenomena of emission wavelength. Based on the results of XPS and FITR, the possible reaction of carbon quantum dots is shown in Fig. 11. The functional groups of CQDs increase with the increase of reaction time. The emission wavelength, thus, slightly shift from blue, green to yellow (see Fig. 12).

Since CQDs is a new genre in luminescent materials that have attracted numerous advertences in last decade, but scientists still confused about the ambiguous PL mechanism of CQDs which might limited further development of CQDs. So far, we have two major hypotheses to clarify the luminescence mechanism of CQDs. One is quantum size effect [40], another is variation of surface state [40]. Both hypotheses direct the reason of luminescence to the energy band gap. In this study, the particle size is about 2.4 nm, luminescent wavelength at 590 nm, compared to others researches listed in Table 3 , it is obvious that the particle size does not influence the emission wavelength greatly but the various carbon sources do. The nature of each carbon sources will decorate miscellaneous functional groups onto the CQDs’ surface during formation of CQDs which made different surface state, and resulting in the controllable energy band gap.

In summary, we exploited ethanol and sodium hydroxide to prepare CQDs through a one step, facile, green and highly reproducible synthetic approach. The obtained CQDs exhibited many traits of controllable emission wavelength, sensitivity of surrounding pH value and great luminescence stability. The emission wavelength of prepared CQDs vary from blue to yellow during reaction period and different surrounding pH condition. From the detailed characterization analysis results of PL, FTIR and XPS to both test of effect of reaction time and pH condition, the outcomes demonstrated that CQDs with difference degree of oxidational functional groups including C=C and –COOH onto surface lead to the variation of emission wavelength by changing the energy band gap of CQDs, thus we conjugate that the red shift phenomena of emission wavelength have a direct relationship to the degree of oxidation of CQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Minister of Science and Technology under contract no. MOST 105-2622-E-033-003-CC2, MOST 106-2112-M-033 -008 and MOST 104-2628-E-033-002-MY3.
